original_text: I think that we should all go vegan! Meat is bad for the earth and for our bodies. First, it takes a lot of water to grow food for animals. Like, did you know it takes 1,000 gallons of water to make just one pound of beef? That's crazy! We could use that water for people who don't have any. And it's not just water, it's also space. Cows take up so much room, and they make so much poop. It's bad for the soil, and it's bad for the air. We should just eat plants instead. They're good for us, and they don't hurt the earth as much. ALUs, plants are yummy! Have you tried tofu? It's so good! And you can put it in anything. My favorite is tofu stir fry with broccoli and carrots. It's so good!

Isn't that astonishing? That water could be used to help individuals facing water scarcity. Furthermore, the land requirements for raising livestock are substantial. Cattle require a significant amount of space, and their waste contributes to soil and air pollution. A plant-based diet, on the other hand, benefits both our health and the environment. Plus, plants can be delicious! Have you ever tasted tofu? It's incredibly versatile and can be incorporated into countless dishes. I personally love tofu stir-fry with broccoli and carrots; it's absolutely delicious.

original_text:
Dear Mr. Senator,

I would like to make a request. Please abandon the idea of the electoral collage. that's a children game. It is not fair game. The electoral collage sends a certain number of electors to represent a state. Why not let an individual person represent his or her self? It is absurd that in Wyoming, a state of 500,000 voters, only get 55 representatives. It is with great activation that I ask you to make this change.
summarized_text:
Dear Senator,

I am writing to you today to urge you to reconsider your support for the Electoral College. With all due respect, I believe it is an outdated and inequitable system that should be abolished. Rather than relying on a system of electors to represent each state, shouldn't each individual have their vote counted directly? It seems particularly unfair that a state like Wyoming, with a population of only 500,000 voters, receives an outsized number of representatives, such as 55. I strongly encourage you to champion a change to this system.

iterations.rewritten_text:
Esteemed Senator,

I pen this missive to implore you: rethink your stance on the Electoral College. Forgive my candor, but I see it as an antiquated, unjust mechanism ripe for abolishment. Instead of this system of electors representing states, why not a direct count of each person's vote? The current setup feels especially lopsided; a state like Wyoming, home to a mere 500,000 voters, wields disproportionate power with its 55 representatives. I earnestly hope you'll spearhead a drive to overhaul this system.

Senator,

I'm writing to ask you to please reconsider your position on the Electoral College. I hope you won't be offended, but it seems like an old and unfair way of doing things that should be gotten rid of. Instead of electors from each state, why can't we just count every single person's vote directly? Right now, it feels very uneven. For instance, a state like Wyoming, which only has about 500,000 voters, has a lot more power than it should with its representatives. I really hope you'll lead the charge to change this system.

I am writing to implore you to rethink your stance on the Electoral College. With all due respect, it appears to be an antiquated and unjust method that warrants abolishment. Why can't we implement a direct popular vote instead of relying on electors from each state? The current system seems inherently unequal. I sincerely hope you will spearhead the effort to reform this flawed system.
